title: Share of adults who are obese vs. daily protein supply
subtitle: Obesity is defined as having a body-mass index (BMI) equal to, or greater than, 30. BMI is a person's weight (in kilograms) divided by their height (in meters) squared.
caption: In 2022, the relationship between daily protein supply and adult obesity rates across countries shows a positive association, where nations with higher per capita protein availability generally report higher percentages of adults with obesity. The scatter plot maps daily protein supply in grams per day per capita along the horizontal axis against the share of adults with obesity on the vertical axis, with points color-coded by region. Countries in Oceania and North America occupy the upper-right quadrant, indicating high values for both metrics; Tonga and Nauru sit near the top of the chart with obesity rates around 70% and protein supplies between 100 and 120 grams, while the United States and Chile show obesity rates between 30% and 45% alongside protein supplies exceeding 120 grams. In contrast, nations in Africa and parts of Asia cluster in the lower-left area, characterized by lower protein availability and lower obesity prevalence; Burundi, Somalia, and Bangladesh appear with protein supplies below 60 grams and obesity rates under 10%. Middle-income nations display varied positions, with countries like Brazil and Mexico showing moderate-to-high obesity rates around 30% with protein supplies near 100 grams, while European nations such as France, Germany, and the United Kingdom group together with protein supplies between 100 and 110 grams and obesity rates ranging from 15% to 25%. The data points form a visible upward trend from the bottom-left to the top-right, showing that as daily protein supply increases, the share of adults with obesity tends to rise across the global dataset.
